source_paragraph: PREVIOUS WORKS Among the relevant published studies in the log-based anomaly detection space, sequence-based is one of the most commonly used approaches. Therefore, there are limited relevant references as this study employs a point-based approach. Below are the relevant baselines from previous research: 1) Pylogsentiment is the first study which employs sentiment analysis-based anomaly detection on operat- ing system logs using GRU and GloVe embedding. 2) SentiLog , similar to , use a two-layered BiLSTM and GloVe embedding model to perform anomaly detection on parallel file system logs. 3) TransSentLog is a further development of and uses a two-layered transformer encoder which used two attention heads and GloVe embedding along with integrated gradients to add explainability to the trained model.

source_paragraph: I. INTRODUCTION I. INTRODUCTION The use of drones has become increasingly common in various applications, including aerial photography, surveying, and monitoring. However, drones can also be involved in incidents such as accidents, violations, or even crimes, making it necessary to investigate drone logs as part of forensic investigations. In a forensic investigation, drone logs are essential sources of information that can help investigators reconstruct the events leading up to an incident. Drone logs typically include information such as flight paths, altitude, speed, battery life, sensor data, and other telemetry information . The choice between one-stage and two-stage object detec- tion models depends heavily on the specific demands of the task, particularly in medical imaging contexts such as colorectal cancer (CRC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), gastric lesions, and polypoid lesions. One-stage detectors, such as YOLO and SSD, excel in speed and computational efficiency, making them ideal for real-time applications. For instance, YOLOv5 demonstrated remarkable performance in CRC detection with nearly perfect metrics—precision (99.071%), recall (98.064%), and mAP (98.8%)—allowing clinicians to analyze colonoscopy videos efficiently during live procedures . Despite the promising results, the model’s generality remains untested as the training dataset contains only log messages acquired from 82 drone models, mostly from DJI. This is one of the main challenges in drone forensics research, where human-readable flight log datasets from other drone models are unavailable. In the future, we plan to explore the flight logs of other drone models to train more general anomaly detection models. The proposed research is based on a simplified deep learn- ing architecture and channel selection approach that leads towards closer and resource-constrained real-time implemen- tation. CONCLUSION This research demonstrates that the YOLOv8 model, specifically the YOLOv8m variant, provides excellent results in detecting Peritoneal Carcinomatosis (PC) from the used laparoscopic image dataset. By utilizing and fine-tuning the pre-trained YOLOv8 weights, this model shows superior performance with a mean Average Precision (mAP50) of 0.871, precision of 0.893, recall of 0.785, and an F1 score of 0.84. The predicted images also show that the performance of YOLOv8 with pre-trained weights is able to predict images well. All YOLOv8 scales are accurate in making predictions, with no errors in image prediction. In contrast, without using pre-trained weights, YOLOv8n and YOLOv8s still make errors in prediction, with some lesions being missed.
